operating_state: Revenue reached $8.29 billion, up 46% year-over-year and 8% sequentially, driven by strong NVIDIA Ampere architecture adoption. Data Center surged from hyperscale customers deploying GPUs for natural language processing and deep recommenders, while Gaming increased on higher GeForce GPU sales and Professional Visualization grew supporting hybrid work environments. Automotive declined due to supply constraints. International sales generated 77% of quarterly revenue with Asia-concentrated manufacturing operations.
cost_structure: Gross margin expanded 140 basis points annually to 65.5%, reflecting higher-end GeForce GPU mix and reduced acquisition-related costs, offset by increased wafer, component, and logistics costs from inflation. Operating expenses surged including a $1.35 billion Arm acquisition termination charge, with additional increases from employee growth and engineering development. Manufacturing lead times extended to twelve months from six months, requiring deposits and premiums to secure future supply capacity.
material_events: NVIDIA terminated the Arm acquisition agreement in February 2022 due to regulatory challenges, recording a $1.35 billion termination cost. The board increased and extended the share repurchase program to $15 billion through December 2023, returning $2.10 billion through repurchases and dividends. Stockholders approved doubling authorized common stock to 8 billion shares and increased equity incentive plan reserves.
macro_exposures: COVID-19 lockdowns in China created supply and logistics constraints impacting end customer sales, with effects continuing into Q2 fiscal 2023. The Ukraine war strained global supply chains and could create foundry material shortages, with Russia representing approximately 2% of end customer sales and direct sales paused. Cryptocurrency market volatility including pending Ethereum changes may decrease GPU mining usage and increase aftermarket resales.
forward_outlook: Management expects continued supply constraints for some products including Networking through Q2 fiscal 2023 and potentially beyond, requiring advance orders with premiums and deposits. Extended twelve-month manufacturing lead times necessitate advance inventory commitments based on long-term demand projections. Supply deliveries may be non-linear within quarters, potentially affecting revenue timing, while the company expects to slow hiring in the second half of fiscal 2023.
uncertainty_profile: Extended twelve-month manufacturing lead times create significant supply-demand mismatch risks and excess inventory exposure, with advanced supply ordering potentially resulting in cancellation penalties if demand reduces. Export control restrictions may target AI-enabling products, potentially limiting market access including China. Tax audits are ongoing in multiple jurisdictions with potential for adverse rate impacts.
